checklist: Analyze the P vs NP Millennium Prize problem
Verify Vertex Cover candidates in graphs
Evaluate bounds of vertex cover checks

states.question: What is the value of the Millennium Prize for proving whether P = NP?
states.explanation: The Clay Mathematics Institute offers a $1,000,000 Millennium Prize for a correct proof solving P vs NP.

states.analogy_description: If P = NP, it means checking a solution (verifying proof) is as easy as finding it. This would break cryptography (RSA keys cracked instantly) but optimize all supply chain grids globally.

whiteboard_data.title: P vs NP Collapse Map
whiteboard_data.description: Complexity classes merging if P equals NP.

steps.details: All NP-complete problems solved in polynomial time!

states.exercise_description: Write a function `is_vertex_cover(graph, vertices)` that checks if the set `vertices` covers all edges in the graph (i.e. every edge in `graph` has at least one endpoint in `vertices`).
Write a greedy vertex cover approximation algorithm `approx_vertex_cover(graph)` that picks edges, adds both endpoints to cover, removes adjacent edges, and returns cover.

states.prompt: Explain The NP-Completeness Challenge in your own words to a peer. Use an analogy from daily life.

flashcards.back: A solution that is at worst twice as large as the optimal solution.

flashcards.front: Is approximation possible for all NP-hard problems?
flashcards.back: No, some problems (like TSP without triangle inequality) cannot be approximated within any constant factor unless P = NP.
